coredata.dc:title: Multi-fluorescent cationic carbon dots for solid-state fingerprinting
coredata.dc:creator: Bahadur, Rohan
Kumawat, Mukesh Kumar
Thakur, Mukeshchand
Srivastava, Rohit
coredata.dc:description: Carbon nanomaterials have been widely explored in bioimaging and biosensing, but their application in forensic science and technology is rare. In this report, we show one-pot synthesis of cationic Carbon Dots (cCDs) and their application in fingerprinting analysis. The cCDs show narrow size distribution (<10 nm), decent quantum yield (c.a. 46%), and a band gap of 2.75 eV. The cCDs exhibit cationic charge and show excitation-dependent multi-fluorescence characteristics. The cCDs show no production of reactive oxygen species and thus are safe to use. Interestingly, they also possess bright liquid as well as solid-state fluorescence. We demonstrate that a well-resolved patterned fingerprint can be obtained within a short time (2–3 min) from different physical surfaces and can be studied using a simple cell phone camera enabling accurate visualization up to third-degree detailing of the fingerprint. Owing to multi-fluorescence characteristics of cCDs, a detailed investigation of the fluorescent patterns was carried out by a three-dimensional multiphoton confocal microscopy. In comparison to traditional methods, the cCDs fluorescence-based fingerprinting shows improvements towards rapid image capture and analysis. Thus, the cCDs form a promising material for rapid fingerprint detection in forensic sciences.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solid-state
Fingerprint
Cell phone
Confocal imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of cCDs in fingerprint detection
Study of fingerprint development using confocal microscopy
Development of fingerprint on various solid surfaces

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) UV spectrum of cCDs with an inset showing the fluorescence of cCDs under UV light (wavelength=375 nm), (b) normalized fluorescence emission spectra recorded at different excitation wavelengths (λ = 250–550 nm), (c) PL spectra (normalized) recorded at different pH values (pH 6–12), (d) photostability analysis of cCDs for 30 min (excitation at 350 nm and emission at 445 nm), (e) solid-state photoluminescence spectrum of cCDs excited at 300 nm, an inset showing fluorescence of lyophilized cCDs under UV light (375 nm), and (f) Tauc plot showing optical band gap of 2.75 eV obtained using absorption spectra.

(a-c) FEG-TEM imaging of ultra-small cCDs, (a) monodispersed cCDs, and the inset shows size distribution analysis wherein, the size ranges from 2 to 10 nm, (b) showing individual QDs of size 2–10 nm, and (c) high-resolution image of individual cCDs including the fringe width. (d) X-ray diffraction pattern, (e) Raman spectrum showing G and D-band, and (f) FTIR spectrum of cCDs.
XPS spectra of cCDs. (a) A wide spectrum of cCDs, and high-resolution deconvulated components for (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s, and (d) O 1s spectra.

(a) Fingerprint development on a non-greasy glass substrate as seen in daylight and UV light (375 nm), (b) The bifurcation and termination of the ridges seen with 4X magnification under an optical inverted microscope, (c) showing a representative greyscale image of a fluorescent fingerprint pattern developed using cCDs. Various features are marked (1−12) with respective color codes. The pattern shows clear differences in the features that can be extracted from the image.

Fingerprint analysis pattern using CLSM and fluorescence analysis using red (543 nm), green (488 nm), and blue (405 nm) lasers. (a) red fluorescence, (b) green fluorescence, (c) blue fluorescence, and (d) merged image of individual fingerprint ridges. The ridges are visible due to cCDs deposited onto them and transferred onto a glass slide. (e) The fluorescent intensity of the individual pattern (shown over an area in (d)) Three-dimensional analysis of the fingerprint ridges formed using cCDs. (f) The X-Z plane of the ridges giving information about the height of the pattern. (g) Orthogonal image showing X-Y plane of three fingerprint ridges, and the depth analysis is shown in (f, h). (h) The width and the spread of fingerprint ridges, which can be used to study the angle and the width to differentiate different fingerprints.

The cCDs-based fingerprint developed on various solid surfaces under UV light (captured using a cell phone camera) and their second-level detailed characterization for termination and bifurcation. The area under green dotted circles denotes bifurcation, and white circles represent termination. Further details and comparison are given in supplementary information (Figs. S8 and S9, Table S3).

head.ce:title: Multi-fluorescent cationic carbon dots for solid-state fingerprinting

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay, 400076 Mumbai, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biosciences and Bioengineering, Indian Institute of Technology Bombay

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon nanomaterials have been widely explored in bioimaging and biosensing, but their application in forensic science and technology is rare. In this report, we show one-pot synthesis of cationic Carbon Dots (cCDs) and their application in fingerprinting analysis. The cCDs show narrow size distribution (<10 nm), decent quantum yield (c.a. 46%), and a band gap of 2.75 eV. The cCDs exhibit cationic charge and show excitation-dependent multi-fluorescence characteristics. The cCDs show no production of reactive oxygen species and thus are safe to use. Interestingly, they also possess bright liquid as well as solid-state fluorescence. We demonstrate that a well-resolved patterned fingerprint can be obtained within a short time (2–3 min) from different physical surfaces and can be studied using a simple cell phone camera enabling accurate visualization up to third-degree detailing of the fingerprint. Owing to multi-fluorescence characteristics of cCDs, a detailed investigation of the fluorescent patterns was carried out by a three-dimensional multiphoton confocal microscopy. In comparison to traditional methods, the cCDs fluorescence-based fingerprinting shows improvements towards rapid image capture and analysis. Thus, the cCDs form a promising material for rapid fingerprint detection in forensic sciences.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon dots (CDs) or graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are fascinating materials owing to their morphological and optical properties [1,2]. These share several similar properties and sometimes it is not easy to distinguish between the two. Their optical properties, biocompatibility and photostablity make them useful and superior to traditional semiconducting QDs for several bio medical applications including forensic science [3,4]. These QDs are chemically rich with high surface area and several oxygen functional groups such as carboxyl, hydroxyl, and epoxy that provide high selectivity and enable chemical interaction with other chemical and biological entities resulting significant changes in optical and other physical properties [5–10]. The presence of differently charged functional groups imparts amphiphilicity to CDs, which is advantageous for detection and quantification of several analytes in healthcare and forensic applications.

Fingerprints are utilized as a primary source of identity in investigation practices, such as crime scenes, fraud cases, or as an evidence, if brought into contact with a substance associated with the incident [11–13]. Fingerprints form due to oil and sweat, which develop an impression on porous and non-porous surfaces. Finger marks contain both the eccrine as well as sebaceous components. While the eccrine part comprises of inorganic compounds, proteins, lipids, amino acids etc., the sebaceous part consists of fatty acids, phospholipids, wax esters and other organic compounds. Therefore, the fingerprints retrieved after evaporation are due to inorganic as well as organic materials present [14,15]. Eccrine is usually a better representation than sebaceous because they are exclusively present on the palm of hands whereas sebaceous can only be visualized if the fingers have touched a part of the body containing hair [16]. However, the fingerprints are not easily visible due to lack of contrast and low resolution of distinct fingerprint ridges. Various techniques including fingerprint powder method (e.g. aluminium dust) [17], chemical treatment using indanediones [18], 1,8 Diazafluoren-9-one [19], and iodine fumes [20] are adopted to extract these fingerprints from solid surfaces. Amongst these, fingerprint powder method, which is rather common, adhere to the sweat and oil component in the fingers but causes contamination in the patterns and loss of valuable information [17]. Further, materials that couple latent fingerprint with molecular recognition can be an effective tool in forensic and diagnostics [21,22]. On the other hand, fluorescent dyes are toxic and generally very expensive. Further, dyes are not photostable and emit in a single wavelength. To alleviate these issues, recently, the quantum dots, in particular, semiconductor quantum dots have attracted significant interest as means of developing fingerprinting techniques because of its color accuracy and color tenability [23], a property that is due to it's small size [24–26]. However, it is well-understood that these quantum dots also suffer from certain disadvantages, such as toxicity and their oxidation upon interaction with air, which would ultimately reduce their fluorescence over a period. This limits the possibilities of the preservation of fingerprints. Hence, these are mostly used in the form of hybrids/composites or in the capped form [27].

Some of the other examples are rare earth nanoparticles [28], and polymer dots [29]. However, these materials are limited by their complicated synthesis procedures, costly and toxic precursors, which could be harmful to the human skin, and low quantum yield. The reproducibility of fingerprints on different surfaces shows contrast decrease because of different background textures and color interference. One of the ways of overcoming contrast deviation is by using compounds with high fluorescence. The highly fluorescent compound coupled with multifluorescent property of CDs can prove to be a viable alternative to conventional fingerprint powder. The quality of fingerprint is lost due to aggregation induced quenching generally observed in fluorescent materials, however, this can be evaded in carbon dots using various known methods [30,31]. Recently, there were a few reports on carbon dots and their composite used for the fingerprinting application [32–36], owing to their biocompatible nature, high quantum yield, and multifluorescent characteristics. Jiang et al. [33] reported fingerprinting application with white emitting CDs on a glass slide, Zhao et al. [34] used CDs/SiO2 composites to examine the stability over a period of six months. They also investigated sensitivity on five different surfaces. None of these reports have extended the study to second level fingerprinting details on multiple surfaces with higher resolution or contrast. Also, Wang et al. [35] reported nitrogen and sulfur doped CDs with decent quantum yield and extended their studies on six different surfaces. However, different levels of visualization are limited on a single surface. To address these issues and advance the scope, we report nitrogen-doped cationic carbon dots (cCDs) as fingerprinting probes as alternative biocompatible solid-state fluorescent emitters for detailed analysis of fingerprint patterns on eleven different surfaces. Interestingly, the fluorescence emission from these patterns upon UV-excitation are quite easily recorded and analyzed using a simple cell phone camera encouraging its application for quick detection in forensic sciences. Further, we have used confocal imaging as a means of quantitative investigation of fingerprint for better reproducibility and standardization.

These cCDs were synthesized using one-pot microwave-assisted synthesis using citric acid as carbon precursor and polyethyleneimine (PEI) as a capping agent which also acts as a fluorescence enhancer [37]. We also note that the cCDs exhibited decent quantum yield (46%) in liquid state and maintained solid-state fluorescence which can be used for fingerprinting application. Fingerprint development and patterning were demonstrated on different surfaces that we encounter in daily routine. The fingerprints were recorded with third level precision within 2–3 min. using a simple cell phone camera, that is rather difficult to analyze with bare eyes.

Citric acid (CA) was purchased from Analytical Rasayan (assay 99–101.0%), Polyethyleneimine (PEI) (50% w/v in water) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich, and Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) was purchased from HiMedia. 2′,7′ –dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFDA) was purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All chemicals were used as purchased.

5 g of citric acid was added to PEI (35%, 500 µL) and PBS (40 mL) and kept for continuous stirring for 10 min. The mixture was then subjected to heating in a microwave oven (180 W) for 30 min. with intermittent addition of PBS until a dark-brown colored precipitate was formed. The precipitate was dispersed in PBS then centrifuged at 15,000 rpm, and the supernatant was collected. The supernatant was filtered using standard Millipore filter (0.22 µm), dialysed for 48 h. The dialysed sample was lyophilized for 48 h to obtain pure cCDs powder. These cCDs were characterized further. The same procedure is followed to synthesize different time-based cCDs by varying the time intervals of 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30 min. under microwave to characterize the step by step formation of cCDs. Similarly, PEI concentration was also optimized for carbon dot preparation by varying volume percentage ratio (v/v%) of PEI:Citric Acid (0%, 2%, 10%, and 50%).

The UV absorption spectrum ( Fig. 1a) shows a peak at 211 nm and a shoulder at 350 nm attributed to π→π * transitions in the C=C bonds and n→π * transitions in the C=O bonds, respectively [1,38]. Photoluminescence studies of cCDs obtained from microwave-assisted pyrolysis of citric acid alone in contrast to PEI-capped CDs at different ratios are shown in Figs. S1 and S2. The PEI-functionalized CDs or the cCDs show a more red-shifted band and a wider full-width half maximum. There is an increase in the fluorescence with increasing PEI concentrations (Fig. S1). However, it was noted that the higher PEI concentrations (>50% v/v) cause precipitation. A typical normalized photoluminescence spectrum of cCDs shows excitation-dependent emission (Fig. 1b) as compared to excitation-independent fluorescence as observed for the sample prepared with citric acid alone [1]. The excitation dependent fluorescence property of cCDs is an important attribute where the fluorescence can be tuned upon excitation at different wavelengths. Additionally, by varying the pH of the cCDs, it is observed that peak does not shift between pH range 6–12 (Fig. 1c). Further optimization regarding photostability, pH stability, and ionic stability is illustrated in Fig. 1d and Fig. S3 and discussed in the supplementary. A time-based synthesis (microwave heating for 5–30 min with an increment of 5 min) in microwave and their fluorescence spectra were recorded. The increase in fluorescence of cCDs with time may be correlated to increasing quantum yield (Fig. S4).

We recorded a solid-state PL spectrum of the cCDs using a thin-film, a broad peak was obtained at 360 nm upon excitation at 300 nm (Fig. 1e). The inset (Fig. 1e) shows bright solid-state fluorescence emission from cCDs powder similar to boron-doped carbon dots reported earlier [39]. The Tauc plot, wherein, the curve was plotted for (αhν)2 v/s hν where α is the absorption coefficient, and hν is the photon energy. The curve was extrapolated by cutting the x-axis at 2.75 eV, which is our experimental optical band gap (Fig. 1f). Citric acid:polyethyleneimine ratio (10:1) was found to be optimum for better stability and fluorescence of cCDs in comparison to other ratios as observed in Fig. S1. Further studies are reported with this optimized composition. The as synthesized cCDs possessed a decent quantum yield of 46% which was measured against quinine sulfate (Q.Y. = 55%) in 0.5 M H2SO4 using a standard procedure [40]. The calculations are given in the supplementary information.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images showed that cCDs, are ultra-small in size in the range of 2–10 nm and the fringe width was approximately 0.157 nm ( Fig. 2a-c) calculated using ImageJ. The zeta potential of cCDs was found to be + 6.64 ± 0.53 mV which is in accordance with the cationic nature of PEI molecules [41]. The XRD pattern in Fig. 2d showed a broad line centered at 20°, assigned as (002). The broadness is attributed to the oxygen defects present in the structure [42–44]. In Fig. 2e, the Raman spectrum with ID/IG ratio 0.94 showed the D-band at 1379 cm−1 usually attributed to the sp3 defects and the G-band at 1592 cm−1 which is related to the in-plane vibrations of sp2 carbon [45]. This indicates that the cCDs contain defects with a partially disordered crystal structure, which could be due to the small sp2 cluster size [46,47]. FTIR spectrum of cCDs (Fig. 2f) shows a broad peak at 3450 cm−1 implying an -OH group, arising from the precursor citric acid present in the cCDs. The shoulder at 2958 cm−1 signifies an alkane C-H stretch. Further, the broad peak at 2534 cm−1 could arise due to the stretching of O-H bonds of carboxylic (-COOH) functional groups. A long sharp peak at 1720 cm−1 signifies a C=O bond stretch and a small narrow peak at 1638 cm−1 which could arise due to C=O bond stretch resulting from –(C=O)-NH-linkage. The peak at 1417 cm−1 indicates the presence of C-N bond stretch in PEI or OH groups present [48].

The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement reveals the functional groups and compositional changes in the cCDs. The wide scan spectra showed a dominant graphitic C 1s peak at 286 eV, and an O 1s peak at 533 eV ( Fig. 3a). Additionally, a peak was found at 400 eV showing the successful doping of cCDs with N-atoms due to the addition of PEI containing NH2 groups. The peaks at 285.5, 286.6 and 289.6 eV correspond to C-C, C-O, and C=O groups, respectively as shown in Fig. 3b [49]. In the Fig. 3c, the peaks at 400.1, 401.2 and 402.6 eV are suggestive of bands due to pyridinic, pyrrolic, and graphitic-nitrogen atoms respectively. Also, the spectra for O 1s showed peaks at 531.6 and 533.1 eV (Fig. 3d) which are assigned to C=O and C-O, respectively[50,51].

To examine the possible toxic effects of cCDs for the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS), we performed a ROS analysis on mouse fibroblasts cells using flow cytometry (Fig. S5). Few reports have shown production of ROS using CDs inside cells [52]. However, the cCDs did not show any ROS production inside the cells even after their incubation with cells for 12 h. Thus, it can be envisaged that the cCDs are biocompatible even if they are internalized inside the skin surface, they are safe for use in healthcare or forensic applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of cCDs in fingerprint detection
ce:section.ce:para: The fingerprint was initially developed on the glass surface, and the fingerprint patterns were visible under UV light (375 nm) which were not easily spotted otherwise ( Fig. 4a). In fingerprint analysis, the patterns and ridges form the first-level details whereas bifurcation and termination [29,53] are considered as some of the second-level details or fingerprint minutiae. The third level details are shown in Fig. 4b and c comprise pores, ridge spaces and scars. The second and third level details form an essential basis of individualization [54]. Using cCDs-based fluorescence emission, we show second-level fingerprint details such as whorl, arch, bridge, island, delta, lake, scar, termination, bifurcation, dot, hook, and pores as seen in Fig. 4c [55]. The third level details are also known as edgeoscopy and poroscopy (Fig. 4c (viii)) which are visible in the optical microscopic image in Fig. 4b and Supplementary Fig. S6. It is believed that the pores are formed due to the sweat pores [56]. These unique details are usually confirmatory signatures for identification of individuals. As the cCDs come in contact with the lipid residues from the fingerprints, a possible effect of lipid on the FTIR spectra of the cCDs may be seen. Further, we carried out FTIR-imaging to understand the interaction of cCDs with the lipids present in the fingerprint (Fig. S7). It is observed that the fingerprints exhibit increased intensity in presence of cCDs. The peaks present at 3295 cm–1, 2956 cm–1, 2580 cm–1, are enhanced in the presence of cCDs and there is no loss of peaks. The peak at 3295 cm–1 signifies N-H stretch from secondary amides, the peak at 1720 cm–1 is a C˭O stretching peak from saturated ester, and the peak at 1638 cm–1 is a C˭O stretching peak from secondary amide (Fig. S7). Further, the C-N stretch peak was seen at 1577 cm–1, and the peak at 1379 cm–1 was probably due to CH3 symmetric bend. The peaks corroborate with the peaks of a latent fingerprint as described in the literature [55]. We could also see from the FTIR imaging that the signal or intensity from the fingerprint was enhanced in presence of cCDs (shown by red color) (Fig. S7b) wherein the spectrum of only fingerprint showed weak intensity (shown by green) (Fig. S7a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of fingerprint development using confocal microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: We further studied the fingerprint pattern under three-dimensional confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM) (561 nm) as illustrated in Fig. 5. The finger pattern was developed and deposited over a glass slide using cCDs solution. The pattern formed is representative of the fingerprint ridges of the individual volunteers. In Fig. 5a-d, the three patterns are distinct and do not interfere with each other. The fluorescence emission shows red, green, and blue colors due to excitation-dependent emission arising from the defects in the cCDs. The fluorescence emission of these patterns is shown in Fig. 5d and quantified in Fig. 5e. The fluorescence intensity data corresponds to the multicolor emission or excitation-dependent characteristics of the cCDs. Usually, the height (depth) information can be used to study the quantitative material deposition on the lipid surface. The cCDs therefore can be used to analyze fingerprint details qualitatively as well as quantitatively using multi-color fluorescence emission of cCDs. The multi-color fluorescence emission is a useful trait in cases wherein different surfaces will give interference in coloration and create problems during read-out. This can be abrogated using multi-color fluorescence emission. A three-dimensional CLSM analysis showed that c.a. 30 µm of the cCDs@lipid has been deposited on the glass surface (Fig. 5f-h). This is an important factor towards the development of a fluorescence detection device with a choice of multiple wavelength selections due to multi-color characteristics and strong emission from cCDs, which was not possible in previous studies [53]. Also, the choice of multiple excitation selection can be useful in cases where the fingerprints got contaminated by blood which itself can possess inherent fluorescence.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development of fingerprint on various solid surfaces
ce:section.ce:para: The fingerprints were developed on ten different solid surfaces, and the patterns or the ridges were found to be distinguishable as seen in the zoomed images ( Fig. 6). The color of the fluorescence of the fingerprints developed on the different surfaces remains same, and this shows that the fluorescent property of the cCDs does not get affected by interaction with the various surfaces having different chemical compositions. These fingerprints were analyzed for second-level details specifically-termination and bifurcation which were distinguishable in all the material surfaces, showing excellent contrast and fluorescence. Other details, such as core, delta, hook, island, and third level details such as, sweat pore and scar were also observed on the surfaces and shown in Figs. S8 and S9. We note from Table S3 that all surfaces except currency observed at least one third level detail apart from various second-level details. We observe from Fig. S10b that the details are best obtained from images having average intensity profile. The high intensity profiles are subjected to the merging of several adjacent ridges that results in less peculiarity (plasticware) whereas the least intensity profile could arise due to lesser intensity of cCDs with the surfaces (porcelain). The surfaces used, vary in nature concerning surface friction, chemical composition, transparency, and infiltration. To study the fluorescent intensity profile of the fingerprints developed on the different surfaces, surface plots were derived from the greyscale images of the prints in the ImageJ software (Figs. S10 and 11 and Table S2). The left column of the each panel in Fig. S11 shows the surface plots and the right column is their respective 3D representation where z-axis was elongated to resolve the print regions. Fig. S11 is discussed in detail in supplementary information.

Thus, the cCDs show a viable alternative to fingerprinting data acquisition and analysis. Since the cCDs are fluorescence-based imaging probes, they do not react or contaminate the evidence at the crime site. We envisage a potential use of these biocompatible nanoprobes for direct application in forensic science.

In summary, we developed cationic carbon dots that can be used for finger printing analysis applications. In contrast to existing fingerprinting technologies, the cCDs are easy to synthesize, biocompatible to use, and provide additional superior solid-state optical properties as probes for fingerprinting analysis using a simple cell phone camera. We envisage that our cCDs will at large foster the development of hand-held detection devices with multicolor detection capability. Also, this may open up the possibilities to explore its application in solving the drug abuse cases where the sweat sample is important for its detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors acknowledge the Sophisticated Analytical Instrumentation Facility (SAIF), Industrial Research Consultancy Centre (IRCC), Indian Institute of Technology Bombay (IITB), for providing central instrumentation facility.

ce:section.ce:para: Supporting information contains detailed experimental materials and methodology, fluorescence optimization of cCDs, optical microscopy, ROS analysis, and comparative fluorescence intensity images and plots of cCDs fingerprint on various solid substrates.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jlumin.2018.12.049.
